serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of Adana Chicken with Mandi Rice weighs approximately 450 g, comprising grilled seasoned ground chicken and a portion of Mandi rice.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Ground Chicken, Cooked (SR Legacy #05298) and Cooked White Rice, Long-Grain (SR Legacy #20045), combined weighted estimate
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Adana Chicken with Mandi Rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by weighting USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked ground chicken (approximately 55% of dish by weight) and cooked long-grain white rice (approximately 45% of dish by weight), with sodium adjusted upward to reflect seasoning and spice blend typical of Adana-style preparation. The input record's per_100g values (energy 166.67 kcal, protein 12.22 g, fat 7.78 g, carbohydrate 11.11 g) are consistent with this weighted estimate and have been retained with cross-validation against USDA data. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are set to -1 where USDA composite data for this specific preparation is not directly available.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Basmati rice, white, boiled (GI 58, analogous to Mandi rice component); combined dish GI estimated by weighting rice and chicken components.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Adana Chicken with Mandi Rice as a composite dish. GI estimated by component weighting: Mandi rice (basmati-style, GI ~58 per University of Sydney GI Database for white basmati rice, boiled) contributes approximately 45% of dish weight; ground chicken component has negligible GI contribution (protein/fat dominant, GI ~0). Weighted composite GI calculated as: (0.45 × 58) + (0.55 × 0) ≈ 26 for the dish as a whole, but since GI is expressed on a carbohydrate-available basis and the dish carbohydrate is 11.11 g/100g, the effective GI of the carbohydrate fraction is dominated by the rice component at GI ~58. A conservative composite GI of 56 is assigned. Glycemic load per 450 g serving: (56 × 11.11 × 4.5) / 100 = 27.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 266.67 mg/100g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt in both the chicken seasoning and Mandi rice preparation, avoid adding high-sodium condiments at the table, and limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate content at 11.11 g/100g with a medium GI of 56; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 50 g of available carbohydrate, which is significant for glycaemic management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request a reduced Mandi rice portion or substitute with a non-starchy vegetable side to lower the glycaemic load, while maximising the lean ground chicken component which provides 12.22 g protein/100g to support satiety and glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 266.67 mg/100g and saturated fat at 2.22 g/100g are the primary cardiovascular concerns; cholesterol at 40 mg/100g is moderate; grilling method avoids excess added fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to manage sodium and saturated fat intake, request reduced salt in preparation, and note that the grilled cooking method and lean ground chicken base are preferable to fried or heavily sauced alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lean white meat (ground chicken) with low arachidonic acid content; garlic and fresh herbs provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols; grilling avoids AGE-generating deep-frying.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume this dish in moderation, ensuring spice blends do not contain large quantities of nightshade-derived spices such as paprika or chilli powder, and limiting the Mandi rice portion to avoid glycaemic-driven inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Adana Chicken with Mandi Rice is a Turkish-inspired grilled chicken dish served in Singapore's food service context, particularly through delivery platforms. The dish features seasoned ground chicken mixed with finely chopped onions, garlic, fresh herbs, and a blend of spices, shaped and grilled on skewers in the style of the traditional Turkish Adana kebab originating from Adana, Turkey. It is served alongside Mandi rice, a fragrant basmati-style rice cooked with aromatic spices in the Middle Eastern Mandi tradition. The chicken is tender, juicy, and mildly spiced with a characteristic smoky char from grilling, while the Mandi rice is fragrant, slightly firm, and subtly spiced. Per 100 g of the combined dish, the nutritional profile includes approximately 166.67 kcal energy, 12.22 g protein, 7.78 g fat (of which 2.22 g is saturated), 11.11 g carbohydrate, 0.89 g dietary fibre, and 266.67 mg sodium. The dish is halal-certified in the Singapore context. Clinically, the moderate sodium content (approximately 1200 mg per 450 g serving) is a consideration for hypertension management, while the medium glycaemic index (~56) and moderate carbohydrate load require portion control for Type 2 Diabetes. The lean ground chicken base makes this a protein-rich option suitable for muscle maintenance. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Adana Chicken with Mandi Rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked ground chicken and cooked long-grain white rice, and are consistent with the input record values. GI is an analogous estimate based on the basmati rice component from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and the absence of a direct approved-source match for this specific dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Adana chicken and Mandi rice dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite preparation is not directly available in approved sources; estimated potassium from chicken and onion components is approximately 230 mg/100g but is not reported as a confirmed value. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16. All core macros (protein_g 12.22, fat_g 7.78, carbohydrate_g 11.11) are non-zero and consistent with a grilled ground chicken and basmati rice composite dish. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate based on the basmati rice component (GI 58, University of Sydney GI Database) weighted by the rice fraction of the dish; this is documented in gi_source. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it is not on the approved source list; USDA FoodData Central was used instead. The dish is classified as Turkish cuisine served in Singapore, and multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per the Singaporean dish multilingual requirement.
